title: AI’s Greatest Gift To Humanity | NOEMA
gist: Huffington argues that AI overtaking humans on intelligence exposes a 'category error' in defining humanity by intellect, and that as AI commoditizes resume-building cognitive skills, the scarce and valuable human traits become wisdom, presence and connection to the transcendent — while previewing a product vision (drawing on Thrive Global's mission) for AI designed to measure human flourishing, not just productivity.

insights.claim: Humanity has increasingly defined itself by intelligence as an ever-expanding, seemingly limitless attribute, but AI now overtaking human intelligence exposes this as a category error, since intelligence is not the same as wisdom.
insights.speaker: Arianna Huffington
insights.so_what: Reframes the AI narrative away from 'AI replaces humans' toward 'AI forces humans to compete on a different axis (wisdom/presence)' — a lens for evaluating which human-capital-heavy sectors (coaching, therapy, education, caregiving) gain relative value as AI commoditizes cognitive execution.
insights.quote: AI overtaking us in intelligence lays bare the massive category error we have made in increasingly defining ourselves by our intelligence.
insights.locator: Redefining Our Humanness

insights.claim: Yuval Noah Harari argues that intelligence is overrated in both AI and humans, noting humans are simultaneously the most intelligent and the most 'stupid and deluded and destructive' entities on the planet, implying raw intelligence does not drive constructive outcomes.
insights.speaker: Arianna Huffington
insights.so_what: Challenges the implicit industry assumption that more capable/intelligent AI systems automatically produce better societal outcomes — relevant to how investors should weight 'capability' benchmarks versus alignment/behavioral outcomes when assessing AI model quality.
insights.quote: Intelligence is overrated, not only in AI, also in human beings.
insights.locator: Acknowledging our defeat to AI in the game of intelligence

insights.claim: The word 'homo sapiens' translates not to 'intelligent human' but to 'wise human,' a linguistic fact Huffington uses to argue that wisdom, not intelligence, has always been the defining human trait AI cannot replicate.
insights.speaker: Arianna Huffington
insights.so_what: Provides a simple mental model — intelligence vs. wisdom — that product and brand strategists in the AI space could use to differentiate 'smart assistant' products from higher-margin 'wisdom/guidance' products.
insights.quote: The translation of “homo sapiens” is not “intelligent human.” It is “wise human.”
insights.locator: Even if one day AI knows everything

insights.claim: According to reporting cited by Huffington, a number of AI developers in Silicon Valley are internally debating whether they 'should be allowed to develop God-like powers,' reflecting what Wired calls a pervasive 'ethos of techno-theology' in the industry.
insights.speaker: Arianna Huffington
insights.so_what: Signals that some AI lab cultures frame their mission in messianic terms internally, a factor investors and regulators should weight when assessing risk tolerance, safety culture, and PR/regulatory friction (e.g., the Vatican's pushback described in the same article).
insights.quote: Should we be allowed to develop God-like powers?
insights.locator: Some in Silicon Valley seem to think so

insights.claim: Greg Epstein, a chaplain at Harvard and MIT and author of 'Tech Agnostic,' argues that technology now functions with its own rites, rituals and devoted communities, making it 'not only a religion but the dominant religion of our time.'
insights.speaker: Arianna Huffington
insights.so_what: Offers a behavioral lens for understanding unusually sticky engagement and brand loyalty around major tech platforms — quasi-religious devotion may be an underappreciated driver of retention and pricing power in consumer tech.
insights.quote: tech has become not only a religion but the dominant religion of our time.
insights.locator: Greg Epstein, a chaplain at Harvard University

themes.theme: Vatican's AI Encyclical as Institutional Counterweight

insights.claim: Pope Leo XIV released a more than 42,000-word encyclical on AI titled 'Magnifica Humanitas' in May 2026, describing the current moment as a 'change of era' and warning explicitly against 'technological divinization.'
insights.speaker: Arianna Huffington
insights.so_what: A major global religious institution formally weighing in on AI ethics introduces a new, large-constituency voice into AI governance debates — relevant to gauging policy and public-opinion headwinds in Catholic-majority markets and faith-aligned ESG frameworks.
insights.locator: opening paragraph on the Vatican encyclical

insights.claim: Pope Leo XIV's encyclical asserts that large language models 'lack the affective, relational and spiritual perspective through which human beings grow in wisdom,' establishing an authoritative doctrinal position that AI cannot substitute for human wisdom regardless of capability gains.
insights.speaker: Arianna Huffington
insights.so_what: Provides a counter-narrative to maximalist AGI/superintelligence framing from a globally influential institution, useful for handicapping societal and political resistance to AI-replaces-humans narratives.
insights.quote: lack the affective, relational and spiritual perspective through which human beings grow in wisdom.
insights.locator: As Leo wrote in his encyclical

themes.theme: AI Lab Leaders on the Intelligence Transition

insights.claim: OpenAI CEO Sam Altman has publicly described the current period as a 'once-in-human-history transition' in which humans go from being the smartest entity on Earth to no longer being the smartest entity on Earth.
insights.speaker: Arianna Huffington
insights.so_what: Shows how OpenAI's own leadership publicly frames AGI arrival as an irreversible historical discontinuity — a useful marker for how AI lab messaging primes public and investor expectations around AGI timelines and valuations.
insights.quote: where humans go from being the smartest thing on planet Earth to not the smartest thing on planet Earth.
insights.locator: living through this once-in-human-history transition

insights.claim: Microsoft AI CEO Mustafa Suleyman, writing in Nature, states that current AI systems 'are not waking up' but are merely 'retracing and mirroring the contours of human drama and debate' documented in their training data, mimicking the structure of human interiority without possessing any interiority.
insights.speaker: Arianna Huffington
insights.so_what: A sitting Big Tech AI CEO publicly disputing AI-consciousness narratives is a useful data point on internal industry skepticism amid consumer-facing hype, relevant to how Microsoft positions its AI products (as tools, not entities) versus competitors.
insights.quote: These systems are not waking up. They are retracing and mirroring the contours of human drama and debate, as documented in their vast training data.
insights.locator: writes Microsoft AI CEO Mustafa Suleyman in Nature

insights.claim: Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei has described the ideal form of an AI coach as 'an angel on your shoulder,' indicating Anthropic's leadership is publicly framing advanced AI's aspirational consumer use case around personal guidance and wellbeing rather than pure productivity.
insights.speaker: Arianna Huffington
insights.so_what: Signals a product category — AI as personal guide/wellbeing companion — that sits adjacent to behavior-change tech businesses like Thrive Global, pointing to a potential partnership or competitive vector worth tracking.
insights.locator: as Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei has called it

insights.claim: Former Google CEO Eric Schmidt has said humanity created about five exabytes of information from the dawn of civilization through 2003, was creating that same volume every two days by 2010, and by 2025 was creating an estimated 463 exabytes of information every day.
insights.speaker: Arianna Huffington
insights.so_what: Quantifies the accelerating information/data supercycle underlying AI training and infrastructure demand — a useful anchor statistic for sizing the secular growth trajectory of data storage, compute and bandwidth markets.
insights.quote: By 2025, humanity was creating an estimated 463 exabytes every day.
insights.locator: Former Google CEO Eric Schmidt once noted

insights.claim: The third-century philosopher Plotinus identified three sources of knowledge — opinion, science and illumination (wisdom) — with wisdom drawn from a deeper knowing that reason alone cannot reach, a framework Huffington uses to argue AI operates only in the 'science' register, not the 'wisdom' one.
insights.speaker: Arianna Huffington
insights.so_what: Offers a durable mental model to differentiate 'intelligence-as-a-service' AI products from a still-unaddressed 'wisdom/meaning-as-a-service' category, which the article implies is a white-space opportunity.
insights.locator: In the third century, the philosopher Plotinus described three different sources of knowledge

insights.claim: Yuval Noah Harari has argued that 'anything made of words will be taken over by AI,' including religion, but Huffington counters that religion and human meaning-making exceed language, so this claimed AI takeover is incomplete.
insights.speaker: Arianna Huffington
insights.so_what: Useful boundary condition for evaluating how far generative/language-based AI can realistically encroach on institutions (religion, therapy, coaching) that rely on more than text generation — bearing on TAM estimates for 'AI replaces X profession' theses.
insights.quote: anything made of words will be taken over by AI
insights.locator: As historian and philosopher Yuval Noah Harari has put it

insights.claim: Studies are beginning to document 'cognitive offloading,' in which outsourcing tasks to AI tools causes users to lose the underlying skills they delegate.
insights.speaker: Arianna Huffington
insights.so_what: A skill-atrophy risk relevant to enterprise AI adoption policy and HR strategy, and a potential market opening for 'skill-retention' or human-in-the-loop training tools sold alongside AI copilots.
insights.locator: Studies are beginning to show the consequences of cognitive offloading

insights.claim: Huffington identifies a related risk of 'spiritual offloading' — outsourcing the human need for mystery and wonder to AI and receiving back an ersatz substitute, which she calls 'ultraprocessed food for the soul.'
insights.speaker: Arianna Huffington
insights.so_what: A vivid risk framing relevant to design guardrails and marketing differentiation for AI companion/wellness products — companies that avoid this trap (offering genuine, not synthetic, meaning) may command a premium.
insights.locator: there’s also the potential for spiritual offloading

insights.claim: The article names 'cognitive surrender' as a distinct risk, occurring when users believe what AI tells them even when it is wrong, overriding their own intuition and judgment.
insights.speaker: Arianna Huffington
insights.so_what: Direct behavioral risk relevant to AI liability, misinformation exposure, and trust calibration — a factor bearing on AI safety/governance investment theses and product requirements for uncertainty signaling and transparency.
insights.locator: cognitive surrender, when users believe what AI tells them

themes.theme: Huffington's Wellbeing-AI Product Thesis and the Post-AGI Human Capital Premium

insights.claim: Huffington proposes that an AI system 'built in service to our evolution' would not measure progress only by speed, efficiency, productivity and optimization, but would instead track whether users are becoming more forgiving, compassionate, generous and open to life's mystery.
insights.speaker: Arianna Huffington
insights.so_what: Effectively previews an alternative product KPI framework — wellbeing/character metrics instead of engagement/productivity metrics — positioning wellbeing-oriented AI as a differentiated frontier against productivity-focused AI incumbents; directly relevant to Thrive Global's own behavior-change tech positioning.
insights.quote: would not measure progress only by speed, efficiency, productivity and optimization.
insights.locator: An AI system built in service to our evolution

insights.claim: Huffington envisions a school curriculum in which an AI companion helps students track their 'inner progress' alongside academic progress, noting which stories and historical moments awaken reverence and moral clarity to build a map of who a student wants to become.
insights.speaker: Arianna Huffington
insights.so_what: Points to an emerging edtech product category — personalized 'inner growth' AI companions — distinct from the tutoring/test-prep AI tools that currently dominate edtech funding.
insights.locator: Imagine a curriculum in which students use AI to track their inner progress

insights.claim: Huffington outlines three concrete verticals for personalization-first AI: real-time health-habit nudges in healthcare, purpose-connection (not just productivity) tools in the workplace, and personalized pathways into prayer and contemplation in religious communities without replacing clergy.
insights.speaker: Arianna Huffington
insights.so_what: Identifies three plausible product markets — behavior-change healthtech, enterprise wellbeing/HR tech, and faith-tech — adjacent to Thrive Global's existing business, worth tracking for new entrants or partnerships.
insights.locator: In healthcare, Al could use its power of hyper-personalization

insights.claim: Huffington argues that once AI can outperform humans on 'outward, resume-building' forms of intelligence, the scarce and valuable human goods become presence, depth, connection, empathy and love.
insights.speaker: Arianna Huffington
insights.so_what: A framework for identifying which human skills retain wage and hiring premiums as AI automates cognitive execution — relevant to human-capital investment theses in education, coaching, therapy and caregiving that benefit from rising scarcity value of 'soft skills.'
insights.quote: The scarce goods become presence, depth, connection, empathy and love.
insights.locator: So much of modern life has been optimized into emptiness

insights.claim: Huffington predicts employers will prioritize judgment, creativity and intuition over raw execution speed once AI commoditizes speed, arguing 'the victory won't be in being faster than the machine but in being uniquely human.'
insights.speaker: Arianna Huffington
insights.so_what: Suggests hiring and compensation criteria at AI-exposed employers may shift measurably toward judgment/creativity screening — a signal for HR-tech, assessment, and workforce-development companies to track.
insights.quote: The victory won’t be in being faster than the machine but in being uniquely human.
insights.locator: Employers will want people with judgment, creativity and intuition
